frameworks.name: Rare & Valuable Skill Stack
frameworks.description: Top 1% in one skill is hard. Top 25% in three complementary skills is achievable and creates rarer combinations. The intersection is where unique value lives.
steps: Inventory current skills — rate each 1-10 vs. general population
Identify your top 3-5 skills at 7+ level
Map intersections: what problems can only someone with THIS combination solve?
Identify the one adjacent skill that would make your current stack 10x more valuable
Invest 80% of learning time in deepening existing stack, 20% in adjacent expansion
Articulate your stack as a unique problem-solving capability, not a list of tools

examples.situation: Engineer who is also a strong communicator and understands business strategy
examples.stack_value: Can translate technical tradeoffs to business decisions — rare intersection commanding premium
examples.next_skill: Sales or negotiation — completes the full value chain from build to sell

antipatterns: Collecting credentials without deepening any single skill to genuine competence
Adding skills that duplicate existing stack rather than create new intersections
Chasing trending skills without connecting them to existing strengths

frameworks.name: Deliberate Practice Protocol
frameworks.description: Naive repetition produces experience, not mastery. Deliberate practice targets specific weaknesses with immediate feedback. Mastery is a function of practice quality, not hours.
steps: Define the specific sub-skill to improve — not 'get better at writing' but 'improve opening paragraphs'
Find or create immediate feedback mechanism — coach, peer review, measurable output
Practice at the edge of current ability — not comfortable repetition
Isolate the weakness: full-context practice embeds errors; isolated drills fix them
Track improvement with concrete metrics — subjective 'feels better' is insufficient
After mastery of sub-skill, reintegrate into full performance
antipatterns: Practicing what you are already good at — reinforces strengths, neglects weaknesses
No feedback loop — practicing errors until they become habits
Volume without specificity — 10,000 hours of naive practice does not produce expertise

frameworks.name: Skill Compounding Timeline
frameworks.description: Skills compound when each new capability multiplies the value of existing ones. The sequence matters as much as the skills themselves.
steps: Map your 3-year skill trajectory: what stack do you want in 36 months?
Identify dependencies: which skills unlock other skills? (Writing unlocks teaching; teaching unlocks leverage)
Sequence acquisition by dependency — foundational skills first
Calculate compounding: Skill A alone = value X. Skill A + B = value 3X. A + B + C = value 10X
Identify your current multiplier skill — the one that amplifies everything else
Protect learning time as non-negotiable — skill compounding requires consistent investment
antipatterns: Random skill acquisition without trajectory — produces breadth without depth or intersection
Stopping at competence rather than continuing to mastery — competent is table stakes, mastery is leverage
No multiplier skill — stacks without amplification compound linearly, not exponentially

prompts.name: Skill Stack Audit
prompts.template: Audit my skill stack. Current skills and self-rated level (1-10): [list]. Career/business goals: [describe]. Identify my current intersection value, the single skill that would most amplify my stack, and my 3-year compounding trajectory.

prompts.name: Deliberate Practice Design
prompts.template: Design a deliberate practice protocol for: [skill]. Current level: [describe]. Specific weakness within this skill: [describe]. Time available: [hours/week]. Give me a targeted practice structure with feedback mechanism and progress metrics.

prompts.name: 3-Year Skill Compounding Plan
prompts.template: Build my 3-year skill compounding plan. Current stack: [list]. Target position/outcome in 3 years: [describe]. Sequence the skill acquisitions by dependency and compounding value, and identify my current multiplier skill.

examples.title: Designer Adding Business Acumen
examples.context: Senior designer at plateau — technically excellent but not advancing
examples.stack_value_before: Design skill alone — executes well, dependent on others for strategy
examples.stack_value_after: Can design AND make the business case — now operates at leadership level
examples.outcome: Moved from senior IC to Head of Design within 18 months

vocabulary.definition: A combination of complementary skills whose intersection creates rare, high-value capabilities not available from any single skill.
Structured practice targeting specific weaknesses with immediate feedback. Produces mastery; naive repetition does not.
A skill that amplifies the value of all other skills in the stack — typically communication, sales, or systems thinking.
Each new skill multiplies rather than merely adds to the value of existing skills when properly sequenced at intersections.
A skill that shares context with existing stack skills, allowing faster acquisition and creating new high-value intersections.
Deep expertise in one area (vertical bar) combined with broad competence across adjacent areas (horizontal bar). Foundation of effective skill stacking.

sources.title: So Good They Can't Ignore You
sources.relevance: Rare and valuable skill development, deliberate practice

sources.title: Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise
Range
sources.relevance: Skill stacking, breadth enabling depth, late specialization advantage

sources.title: How to Fail at Almost Everything and Still Win Big

sovereign_needs_served: Legacy (compounded expertise outlasts any single role)
